name: Fergus mac Roich
description: Fergus mac Róich is a prominent hero in the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology. He was a renowned warrior and king of Ulster, known for his immense strength, generosity, and, perhaps most notably, his extraordinarily large size and appetite. He was betrayed by Conchobar mac Nessa, who tricked him out of his kingship. Fergus, in his honorable nature, kept his word despite the deception, leading to his exile and eventual alliance with Queen Medb of Connacht. He became a loyal and valued member of Medb's court and a key figure in the Táin Bó Cúailnge (Cattle Raid of Cooley), where he led the Connacht army against Ulster. Despite fighting against his former kingdom, Fergus's loyalty to Medb never wavered. He is often depicted as a wise and powerful leader, though sometimes prone to impulsive decisions influenced by his appetites. His sword, Caladbolg, was said to be capable of cleaving hills in two. Fergus's story is a complex tale of honor, betrayal, exile, and loyalty, making him a compelling figure in Irish folklore. His immense size and strength are recurring themes, and he's often portrayed as a jovial giant with a powerful presence. His legacy endures as a symbol of both heroic strength and the tragic consequences of misplaced trust.
subtitle: Giant King of Ulster, Exiled Hero
achievements: Leader in the Táin Bó Cúailnge

keyMyths.title: The Kingship of Ulster
keyMyths.description: Fergus was tricked out of the kingship of Ulster by Conchobar mac Nessa, leading to his exile. He honored his word to step down after a year, despite realizing the deception.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Ulster Cycle

keyMyths.title: The Táin Bó Cúailnge
keyMyths.description: Fergus led the Connacht army against Ulster in the Cattle Raid of Cooley, displaying his military prowess. He was a crucial figure in Medb's campaign to steal the Brown Bull of Cooley.

keyMyths.title: Caladbolg
keyMyths.description: Fergus's sword, Caladbolg, was a powerful weapon capable of cleaving through hills. It was a symbol of his strength and status as a warrior.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Ulster Cycle

sources.author: Thomas Kinsella
sources.description: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge.
sources.text: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge.

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz
sources.description: A collection of translated Irish myths and sagas.
sources.text: A collection of translated Irish myths and sagas.
